blocks:
Ayahs

تَبَّتْ يَدَآ أَبِى لَهَبٍ وَتَبَّ (١)
Perish the hands of Abu Lahab, and perish he! (1)
مَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُ مَالُهُۥ وَمَا كَسَبَ (٢)
Neither his wealth availed him, nor what he had earned. (2)
سَيَصْلَىٰ نَارًا ذَاتَ لَهَبٍ (٣)
Soon he will enter the blazing fire, (3)
وَٱمْرَأَتُهُۥ حَمَّالَةَ ٱلْحَطَبِ (٤)
and his wife [too], the firewood carrier, (4)
فِى جِيدِهَا حَبْلٌ مِّن مَّسَدٍۭ (٥)
with a rope of palm fibre around her neck. (5)

(Commentary)

This is a severe threat directed at Abu Lahab regarding the destruction of his person and his deeds, and the fire of Hell, as well as a threat to his wife. The Surah is Meccan.

His saying, the Exalted: تَبَّتْ يَدا أَبِي لَهَبٍ وَتَبَ — Perish the hands of Abu Lahab, and perish he! (111:1). Loss (التب) and ruin (التباب) refer to loss and destruction according to what al-Jawhari mentioned, or the persistence of loss according to what al-Raghib mentioned. It has also been said to mean disappointment, or being void of all good. These meanings—as has been said—are closely related. The hand of a human being is the limb through which he attains his objectives, and to it most of his actions are attributed. Thus, the ruin (تباب) of his two hands is their loss in what they earn of deeds; or if you wish, you could say: the nullification of the deeds he performs with them, in that they do not reach a desired goal and he derives no benefit from any of them. The ruin (تباب) of his person is the loss within his own soul by being deprived of eternal happiness, which is his everlasting destruction.

Thus, His saying: تَبَّتْ يَدا أَبِي لَهَبٍ وَتَبَ —meaning Abu Lahab—is an invocation against him for the destruction of his person and the nullification of the deeds he used to perform to extinguish the light of Prophethood, or it is a decree (قضاء) from Him, the Exalted, to that effect.

This Abu Lahab is Abu Lahab ibn Abd al-Muttalib, the uncle of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. He was intensely hostile toward the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, persistent in calling him a liar, and excessive in harming him with whatever speech and action he could muster. He was the one who said to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "Perish you!" (تبا لك) when he called them to Islam for the first time; so the Surah was revealed, and Allah returned the ruin (التباب) upon him.

Some have mentioned that "Abu Lahab" was his name, even if it takes the form of a patronymic (الكنية). It has also been said his name was Abd al-Uzza, or Abd Manaf. The best explanation for mentioning him in the verse by his patronymic rather than his name is that it serves as a form of mockery (تهكم) against him. This is because "Abu Lahab" (Father of Flame) suggests an association with the flame (لهب) of the Fire, just as one says "Abu al-Khayr" (Father of Good), "Abu al-Fadl" (Father of Virtue), or "Abu al-Sharr" (Father of Evil) in association with good, virtue, or evil. Thus, when it was said: سَيَصْلى ناراً ذاتَ لَهَبٍ — He shall enter a fire of flaming sparks (111:3), it was understood that His saying: تَبَّتْ يَدا أَبِي لَهَبٍ carries the meaning of our saying: "Perish the hands of a denizen of Hell who is inseparable from its flame."

It has also been said that he was not mentioned by his name, which was Abd al-Uzza, because "Uzza" is the name of an idol, and it was disliked to count him—in terms of the wording—as a servant (عبد) of other than Allah, when he is in fact a servant of Allah, even if the name is only intended to identify the named person.

His saying, the Exalted: ما أَغْنى عَنْهُ مالُهُ وَما كَسَبَ — His wealth did not avail him, nor what he earned (111:2). The first ma (ما) is negatory, and the second ma (ما) is a relative pronoun.

The meaning of ما كَسَبَ is that which he earned through his deeds—which is the effect of his actions—or it is a gerund (مصدرية), meaning "his earning with his hands," which is his action itself. Thus, the meaning is: "his deeds did not avail him."

In any case, the meaning of the verse is that neither his wealth nor his deeds—or the effects of his deeds—could ward off the ruin (تباب) of his person and his hands that was decreed for him or invoked against him.

His saying, the Exalted: سَيَصْلى ناراً ذاتَ لَهَبٍ — He shall enter a fire of flaming sparks (111:3). That is, he shall enter a fire possessing flames, which is the eternal fire of Hell. The use of the indefinite form for "flame" (لهب) serves to magnify (تفخيم) and make it more terrifying (تهويل).

His saying, the Exalted: وَامْرَأَتُهُ حَمَّالَةَ الْحَطَبِ — And his wife, the firewood carrier (111:4). This is conjoined to the latent subject pronoun in "he shall enter" (سيصلى). The implied meaning is: "and his wife shall enter, etc." The phrase "the firewood carrier" (حَمَّالَةَ الْحَطَبِ) is in the accusative case (النصب) as a description cut off from its adjectival function for the purpose of dispraise (الذم); meaning: "I dispraise the firewood carrier." It has also been said to be a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for "his wife," which is a subtle meaning as will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: فِي جِيدِها حَبْلٌ مِنْ مَسَدٍ — With a palm-fiber rope around her neck (111:5). The term al-masad (المسد) refers to a rope twisted from fiber. This sentence is a second circumstantial qualifier (حال) for his wife.

It appears that the intent of the two verses is that she will be manifested in the Fire she enters on the Day of Resurrection in the same form she assumed in the world. This is because she used to carry thorny branches and the like, throwing them at night in the path of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to harm him. Thus, she will be punished in the Fire while carrying firewood with a palm-fiber rope around her neck.

It is stated in Majma' al-Bayan: "If it is asked: Was Abu Lahab required to believe after this Surah was revealed? And was he capable of believing? For if he had believed, it would have necessitated the falsification of Allah's report that he would enter a fire of flaming sparks."

The answer is that belief was required of him because the obligation of faith remained upon him. Allah only threatened him with the condition that he would not believe. (End of the relevant portion).

I say: The basis of this problem lies in the oversight that the attachment of the definitive decree (القضاء الحتمي) from Him, the Exalted, to a human being's voluntary action (الفعل الاختياري) does not necessitate the nullification of choice (الاختيار) or the compulsion of the human being toward the action. For the Divine Will (الإرادة الإلهية)—as well as His act—only attaches to the voluntary action as it is; that is, that the human being performs such-and-forth by his own choice. If the action did not occur by choice, His intended purpose would fail to match His will, which is impossible. Since the action to which the decree is attached is voluntary, its abandonment is also voluntary, even if it does not occur. So understand this. This discussion has preceded in several places in the previous inquiries.

Thus, it has become clear that it was within Abu Lahab's choice to believe and thereby be saved from the Fire, which was a settled decree that he would enter due to his disbelief.

Of this category are the verses revealed regarding the disbelievers of the Quraysh stating that they would not believe, such as His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ — As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith (2:6), and His saying: لَقَدْ حَقَّ الْقَوْلُ عَلى أَكْثَرِهِمْ فَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ — The word has certainly become due against most of them, so they will not have faith (36:7). Also of this category are the verses regarding the sealing (الطبع) of hearts.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ — Warn your nearest relations (26:214), narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "When this verse was revealed, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله climbed al-Safa and called out: 'O people, come quickly!' (يا صباحاه). The Quraysh gathered to him and said: 'What is the matter with you?' He said: 'Do you see that if I told you that the enemy was going to attack you in the morning or the evening, would you believe me?' They said: 'Yes.' He said: 'Then I am a warner to you before a severe punishment.' Abu Lahab said: 'Perish you! Is it for this that you have gathered us all?' Then Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, revealed: تَبَّتْ يَدا أَبِي لَهَبٍ — Perish the hands of Abu Lahab (111:1)."

I say: It was also narrated in the commentary of the Surah from Sa'id ibn Jubayr from Ibn Abbas, but it did not mention that the call was at the time of the revelation of the verse: وَأَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ — Warn your nearest relations.

In the same source, narrated from Tariq al-Muharibi, who said: "While I was at the market of Dhu al-Majaz, I saw a young man saying: 'O people, say: There is no god but Allah, and you will succeed.' And there was a man behind him throwing stones at him until he made his shins and heels bleed, saying: 'O people, he is a liar, so do not believe him.' I asked: 'Who is this?' They said: 'This is Muhammad, who claims to be a prophet, and this is his uncle Abu Lahab, who claims he is a liar.'"

In Qurb al-Isnad, with its chain of narration to Musa ibn Ja'far عليه‌السلام in a long tradition mentioning the signs of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, he said: "Among them is that Umm Jamil, the wife of Abu Lahab, came to him when the Surah Tabbat was revealed. With the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was Abu Bakr ibn Abi Quhafah. He said: 'O Messenger of Allah, here is Umm Jamil, enraged (محفظة)—meaning angry—seeking you, and she has a stone with which she wants to pelt you.' He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'She will not see me.' She said to Abu Bakr: 'Where is your companion?' He said: 'Where Allah wills.' She said: 'I have come to him, and if I saw him, I would have pelted him, for he has lampooned me. By al-Lat and al-Uzza, I am indeed a poetess!' Abu Bakr said: 'O Messenger of Allah, did she not see you?' He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'No, Allah placed a veil between me and her.'"

I say: Something similar has been narrated through several Sunni chains of transmission.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَامْرَأَتُهُ حَمَّالَةَ الْحَطَبِ — And his wife, the firewood carrier (111:4), he said: "She was Umm Jamil bint Sakhr. She used to spread slander (تنم) against the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and carry his reports to the disbelievers."
